news_summary: Recent advancements showcase breakthrough materials with rapid adaptability, promising near-error-free quantum computing, innovative practical tools, and significant progress toward highly intelligent robots.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes mettent en avant des matériaux révolutionnaires dotés d'une grande adaptabilité, laissent entrevoir un calcul quantique pratiquement sans erreur, proposent des outils pratiques innovants et marquent des progrès significatifs vers la mise au point de robots hautement intelligents.
news_summary_es: Los avances recientes ponen de manifiesto materiales revolucionarios con una rápida adaptabilidad, que prometen una computación cuántica prácticamente libre de errores, herramientas prácticas innovadoras y avances significativos hacia la creación de robots altamente inteligentes.

articles.title: This strange material can become strong or fall apart in seconds
articles.summary: Scientists have found that staple-shaped particles can tangle together to create a material that is both strong and flexible. Unlike conventional materials, these particles can be locked into a sturdy structure or rapidly unraveled using vibrations. The unusual behavior could open the door to recyclable buildings, reconfigurable structures, and even futuristic robotic technologies.
articles.content:
A tightly compressed bundle of office staples can behave in a surprising way. Even though it is made of many separate pieces, the tangled mass can be difficult to pull apart and can act almost like a single solid object.

Yet that same bundle can quickly come undone. With the right vibration or movement, the staples can separate and return to a loose collection of individual pieces.

Researchers at the Paul M. Rady Department of Mechanical Engineering at CU Boulder believe this unusual combination of strength and reversibility could help inspire a new generation of engineered materials. By designing particles that interlock in a similar way to staples, they hope to create materials that are strong, adaptable, and potentially recyclable.

"We've been playing around with the idea of building blocks and geometry for many years, but we started looking at interlocking, entangled particles only recently," said Professor Francois Barthelat, the leader of the Laboratory for Advanced Materials & Bioinspiration. "We are excited about the combination of properties we can get out of these systems and we believe this technology has the potential to go in many directions."

The findings were recently published in the Journal of Applied Physics.

How Entangled Particles Create Strength

The research centers on a phenomenon known as entanglement, which occurs when particles become intertwined and form connections with one another.

Entanglement is common throughout nature. Bird nests, for example, rely on a network of interwoven twigs and fibers to maintain their structure. Bones also gain strength through the interaction of hard mineral components and softer proteins.

The CU Boulder team wanted to understand how similar principles could be used to create manufactured materials. Their work pointed to one crucial factor: the shape of the particles themselves.

"Let's take sand as an example. Sand is smooth and convex-shaped, meaning it cannot interlock from grain to grain," PhD student Youhan Sohn said. "However, we found that if we change the shape of a grain of sand, we can drastically affect its behavior and mechanical properties, including the particle's ability to link with other particles."

To investigate further, the researchers used Monte Carlo simulations, a computational technique that allowed them to study how different particle shapes interact. Their objective was to identify a geometry that would maximize entanglement.

Why Staple-Shaped Particles Stand Out

After identifying promising designs through simulation, the team conducted pickup tests to observe how the particles behaved in real-world conditions.

The results revealed that a "two-legged" particle, resembling a staple, produced the highest degree of entanglement. The researchers also found that this shape offered several unexpected benefits.

One of the most notable was its ability to combine tensile strength and toughness, two properties that are often difficult to achieve together in conventional materials.

"Our entangled granular material using the staple-like particle demonstrates both high strength and toughness at the same time," said PhD student Saeed Pezeshki.

The staple-like particles also displayed another unusual characteristic. They could rapidly come together into a stronger structure and then just as quickly separate again.

By applying different vibration patterns, the researchers were able to control how strongly the particles became entangled. Gentle vibrations encouraged the particles to interlock and strengthen the material, while stronger vibrations caused the network to unravel.

"It's a strange material because it's obviously not a liquid. However, it's also not quite solid. This opens new and intriguing engineering possibilities," Barthelat said. "Handling a bundle of these entangled particles feels very remote and exotic."

Potential Uses in Construction and Robotics

The researchers believe the technology could eventually support more sustainable approaches to construction.

In the future, bridges, buildings, and other large structures might be built using entangled materials that can later be taken apart rather than demolished. Such materials could potentially be reused or fully recycled at the end of their service life.

The concept may also have applications in robotics.

"I was talking with other students who believe this technology can be used in swarm robotics -- where small robots can entangle, do a task and then disentangle when they are done," said Pezeshki.

"Yes, kind of like that liquid metal T-1000 in Terminator 2 who can change shape to slide under a door and then transform back to a human's size on the other side," added Barthelat. "It's expensive and scaling up is a challenge, but it's something that's on everybody's mind."

Testing Even Stronger Particle Designs

The team is now moving into the next stage of the research.

Their latest experiments focus on a new particle design that includes additional protruding "legs." The researchers compare the shape to the spiky burrs that cling stubbornly to shoes and clothing outdoors. They believe these added features could create even stronger entanglement effects and unlock new possibilities for future materials.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant scientific breakthrough in engineered materials using staple-shaped particles that can rapidly switch between strong and flexible states. This discovery has broad potential applications in sustainable construction and robotics, offering tangible benefits for society and the environment. The article provides detailed context on the research, its mechanisms, and future implications, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create material that can switch from strong to loose in seconds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists at the University of Colorado Boulder studied how particles shaped like staples can tangle together. This idea comes from nature, where things like bird nests or bones gain strength by parts connecting tightly.

**What happened** – Researchers found that staple-shaped particles can lock tightly to form a strong material. But when vibrations are applied, these particles quickly come apart and become loose again. This discovery was made using computer simulations and real tests.

**Impact** – This material is unusual because it can be both strong and flexible, which is hard to achieve with normal materials. It could lead to buildings or bridges that can be taken apart and reused instead of destroyed. It might also help make robots that can change shape by locking and unlocking their parts.

**What's next step** – The team is now testing new particle shapes with extra legs, like burrs that stick to clothes, to create even stronger connections. This could lead to materials with new abilities and uses in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Staple-shaped particles can create materials that switch quickly between strong and loose, offering new ideas for recycling and robotics.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent un matériau qui passe de solide à lâche en quelques secondes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des chercheurs de l'Université du Colorado à Boulder ont étudié des particules en forme d'agrafes qui peuvent s'emmêler. Cette idée vient de la nature, où des choses comme les nids d'oiseaux ou les os gagnent en solidité grâce à des connexions serrées.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Les chercheurs ont découvert que ces particules en forme d'agrafes peuvent se verrouiller pour former un matériau solide. Mais avec des vibrations, ces particules se séparent rapidement et redeviennent lâches. Cette découverte a été faite grâce à des simulations informatiques et des tests réels.

**Impact** – Ce matériau est spécial car il peut être à la fois solide et flexible, ce qui est difficile à obtenir avec des matériaux classiques. Il pourrait permettre de construire des bâtiments ou des ponts démontables et réutilisables au lieu d'être détruits. Il pourrait aussi aider à fabriquer des robots capables de changer de forme.

**Prochaine étape** – L'équipe teste maintenant de nouvelles formes de particules avec des « pattes » supplémentaires, comme les bardanes qui collent aux vêtements, pour créer des connexions encore plus solides. Cela pourrait ouvrir la voie à des matériaux aux capacités inédites.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des particules en forme d'agrafes peuvent créer des matériaux qui passent rapidement de solides à lâches, offrant de nouvelles idées pour le recyclage et la robotique.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean un material que cambia de fuerte a suelto en segundos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Colorado en Boulder estudiaron partículas con forma de grapas que pueden enredarse. Esta idea viene de la naturaleza, donde cosas como los nidos de pájaros o los huesos ganan fuerza al conectarse firmemente.

**Qué pasó** – Los científicos descubrieron que estas partículas en forma de grapas pueden bloquearse para formar un material fuerte. Pero con vibraciones, las partículas se separan rápidamente y vuelven a estar sueltas. Esto se descubrió usando simulaciones por computadora y pruebas reales.

**Impacto** – Este material es especial porque puede ser fuerte y flexible al mismo tiempo, algo difícil de lograr con materiales normales. Podría usarse para construir edificios o puentes que se puedan desmontar y reutilizar en lugar de destruir. También podría ayudar a crear robots que cambien de forma.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo está probando nuevas formas de partículas con más “piernas”, como las bardanas que se pegan a la ropa, para hacer conexiones más fuertes. Esto podría llevar a materiales con nuevas capacidades.

**Resumen en una frase** – Partículas en forma de grapas pueden crear materiales que cambian rápido entre fuerte y suelto, ofreciendo nuevas ideas para reciclaje y robótica.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a tightly tangled cluster of staple-shaped particles interlocking like a strong, flexible mesh, with gentle vibrations shown as soft, flowing waves causing parts of the cluster to loosen and separate, symbolizing strength and adaptability through entanglement.

articles.title: Are useful and error-free quantum computers only two years away?
articles.summary: Quantum computing firm QuEra says it plans to make a fault-tolerant quantum computer and offer it to users through the cloud in 2028, which will require a real leap in engineering
articles.content:
Quantum computing firm QuEra says it plans to make a fault-tolerant quantum computer and offer it to users through the cloud in 2028, which will require a real leap in engineering

A prototype of the Libra quantum computer QuEra

Useful, error-free quantum computers could be ready in just two years, researchers at the quantum computing firm QuEra have claimed.

Quantum computing is a quickly maturing technology, and the industry surrounding it is growing rapidly. The biggest obstacle to today’s quantum computers having uses in chemistry, materials and drug development lies in how error-prone they are, which limits the complexity of computations they can run. Now, Yuval Boger at QuEra and his colleagues are confident that they will soon bypass this obstacle.

Libra, the firm’s fault-tolerant quantum computer – a computer that can catch and correct its own errors – is slated to arrive in 2028 and be available through the cloud through a collaboration with Amazon Web Services (AWS). Currently, a fully functional fault-tolerant quantum computer doesn’t exist. “Having the first fault-tolerant quantum computer will be like breaking the sound barrier,” says Boger.

The basic components of Libra, which are called qubits, will be made from extremely cold, electrically neutral atoms controlled with lasers. It will contain between 10,000 and 15,000 qubits, which will be partitioned into 256 groups called logical qubits, each of which will commit an error only once in a million times, even if individual qubits’ reliability within those groups is lower.

QuEra’s researchers project that this will enable Libra to run a “megaquop” or 1 million operations. In 2025, quantum computing expert John Preskill at the California Institute of Technology told New Scientist that a megaquop machine could usher in a new era for quantum computing. But the leap that QuEra’s team must make to deliver on this promise is high. Currently, the largest array of neutral atom qubits contains 6100 of them, but they haven’t yet been used for computations. The largest number of error-corrected logical qubits made so far is 48. Industry giants such as IBM predict that they will be able to offer fault-tolerant quantum computers starting in 2029.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Daily The latest on what’s new in science and why it matters each day. Sign up to newsletter

Jonathan King at Atom Computing, which also makes neutral atom quantum computers, says that a fully functional computing system, as opposed to a laboratory demonstration, will require integrating many scientific and engineering advances. According to Boger, QuEra is currently running five experimental machines to perfect different aspects of Libra, such as replacing atoms that become faulty by getting warm, or managing the power of the large number of lasers needed, and to combining them.

“The balance has shifted from 90 per cent science to 10 per cent engineering to tipping more towards engineering,” says Boger. The firm’s scientists are also working on improving how conventional computers are used to control and monitor the qubits, as well as collaborating with the team at AWS on integrating Libra into the company’s conventional cloud infrastructure.

“There is still a lot to do,” says Thomas Wong at Creighton University in Nebraska. “It’s plausible that they’ll get there by 2028, but it’s equally plausible that they’ll overshoot it by a couple of years or more.” Joe Fitzsimons at Horizon Quantum Computing says that the plan for Libra is certainly ambitious, but that the firm has already been involved in breakthroughs concerning error correction for quantum computers, so it has a strong track record. There are several different ways to make qubits, but the neutral atom approach lends itself to an easier conversion between qubits and logical qubits, which could prove to be a key advantage, he says.

Even if all goes according to plan, one question will remain: what can a megaquop machine do? Boger says it will be well-suited for complex simulations of systems from physics and materials science that are currently too difficult for conventional and existing quantum computers. And he hopes that researchers will use it to develop better quantum computing algorithms for the next generation of fault-tolerant machines. “I wouldn’t be surprised if most algorithms that end up being useful haven’t been discovered yet,” says Boger.

In Wong’s view, Libra stands a better chance of being a discovery machine more launching game-changing applications. “I’d say QuEra is hoping to shape the community’s research direction to help them figure out what to do with their 256 logical qubits,” he says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant technological breakthrough in quantum computing, with QuEra aiming to develop the first fault-tolerant quantum computer by 2028. This advancement promises to overcome current limitations of error-prone quantum computers, potentially enabling complex simulations and new algorithm discoveries that could broadly benefit science and technology. The story has clear substance, focusing on a single major development with broad implications for the future of computing and research.
articles.personality_title: QuEra aims to launch first error-correcting quantum computer by 2028
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers are special machines that can solve problems much faster than regular computers, but today’s versions make many errors. These mistakes stop them from being useful for important tasks like discovering new medicines or materials.

**What happened** – A company called QuEra plans to build a new type of quantum computer named Libra that can fix its own errors. They say Libra will be ready by 2028 and users can access it through the internet with help from Amazon Web Services. Libra will use super-cold atoms controlled by lasers as its building blocks, called qubits, and will have thousands of them working together.

**Impact** – This is important because current quantum computers can only handle small tasks before errors ruin the results. Libra’s design aims to reduce errors drastically, allowing it to perform a million operations reliably. This could open the door to solving complex science problems that regular computers cannot, like simulating new materials or physical systems.

**What's next step** – QuEra is testing parts of Libra right now, working on challenges like keeping atoms cold and managing many lasers. They are also working with Amazon to connect Libra to cloud services so many people can use it. Experts say the goal is ambitious but possible, and Libra might help scientists discover new ways to use quantum computers.

**One-sentence takeaway** – QuEra’s planned Libra quantum computer could be the first to correct its own errors, making powerful quantum computing accessible by 2028 through the cloud.
articles.personality_title_fr: QuEra prévoit de lancer le premier ordinateur quantique corrigant ses erreurs d’ici 2028
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques sont des machines spéciales capables de résoudre certains problèmes beaucoup plus rapidement que les ordinateurs classiques, mais les versions actuelles font beaucoup d’erreurs, ce qui limite leur utilité pour des tâches importantes comme la découverte de médicaments ou de nouveaux matériaux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une entreprise appelée QuEra prévoit de construire un nouvel ordinateur quantique nommé Libra, capable de corriger ses propres erreurs. Ils annoncent que Libra sera prêt en 2028 et accessible via internet grâce à Amazon Web Services. Libra utilisera des atomes ultra-froids contrôlés par des lasers comme éléments de base, appelés qubits, et en comptera des milliers travaillant ensemble.

**Impact** – C’est important car les ordinateurs quantiques actuels ne peuvent gérer que de petites tâches avant que les erreurs n’en ruinent les résultats. Le design de Libra vise à réduire drastiquement ces erreurs, ce qui lui permettra d’exécuter un million d’opérations de manière fiable. Cela pourrait ouvrir la voie à la résolution de problèmes scientifiques complexes que les ordinateurs classiques ne peuvent pas traiter, comme la simulation de nouveaux matériaux ou systèmes physiques.

**Prochaines étapes** – QuEra teste actuellement des parties de Libra, en travaillant sur des défis comme le maintien des atomes froids et la gestion de nombreux lasers. Ils collaborent aussi avec Amazon pour connecter Libra aux services cloud afin que beaucoup de personnes puissent l’utiliser. Des experts jugent cet objectif ambitieux mais possible, et Libra pourrait aider les scientifiques à découvrir de nouvelles façons d’utiliser les ordinateurs quantiques.

**Phrase-clé** – L’ordinateur quantique Libra de QuEra pourrait être le premier à corriger ses propres erreurs, rendant l’informatique quantique puissante accessible dès 2028 via le cloud.
articles.personality_title_es: QuEra planea lanzar la primera computadora cuántica que corrige errores para 2028
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras cuánticas son máquinas especiales que pueden resolver ciertos problemas mucho más rápido que las computadoras normales, pero las versiones actuales cometen muchos errores, lo que limita su uso para tareas importantes como descubrir nuevos medicamentos o materiales.

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa llamada QuEra planea construir una nueva computadora cuántica llamada Libra que puede corregir sus propios errores. Dicen que Libra estará lista para 2028 y que los usuarios podrán acceder a ella a través de internet con la ayuda de Amazon Web Services. Libra usará átomos súper fríos controlados por láseres como sus partes básicas, llamadas qubits, y tendrá miles de ellos trabajando juntos.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque las computadoras cuánticas actuales solo pueden hacer tareas pequeñas antes de que los errores arruinen los resultados. El diseño de Libra busca reducir mucho esos errores, permitiéndole realizar un millón de operaciones de forma confiable. Esto podría abrir la puerta para resolver problemas científicos complejos que las computadoras normales no pueden, como simular nuevos materiales o sistemas físicos.

**Próximo paso** – QuEra está probando partes de Libra ahora mismo, enfrentando desafíos como mantener los átomos fríos y manejar muchos láseres. También trabajan con Amazon para conectar Libra a servicios en la nube para que muchas personas puedan usarla. Los expertos dicen que la meta es ambiciosa pero posible, y Libra podría ayudar a los científicos a descubrir nuevas formas de usar computadoras cuánticas.

**Frase clave** – La computadora cuántica Libra de QuEra podría ser la primera en corregir sus propios errores, haciendo que la computación cuántica poderosa esté disponible para 2028 a través de la nube.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing cluster of thousands of delicate, interconnected luminous atoms suspended in a dark space, each atom linked by fine beams of light symbolizing laser control, with subtle abstract geometric patterns representing error correction and quantum entanglement gently weaving through the cluster, evoking a sense of advanced quantum technology poised for breakthrough without any human figures or text.

articles.title: In the field — and in the lab — sometimes the simplest tool is best
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 15 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01885-yTechnology has led to unprecedented innovation, but common household items can help make research more reproducible — and accessible.
articles.content:
A basic toolkit can be useful for coming up with creative solutions to technological hurdles.Credit: skodonnell/Getty

For David Thomas, a walk to the kitchen for coffee is a trip down memory lane. Standing amid the spoons, pans and pantry goods of his home, Thomas — an Arctic researcher at the University of Helsinki in Finland — is reminded of days spent on the ice, swaddled in layers of clothing that keep him alive at temperatures as low as −40 °C. That’s because when some of these kitchen staples are not being used for their usual duties, Thomas brings them on expeditions as crucial components of his field kit.

A DIY approach to automating your lab

Thomas studies the biogeochemistry of aquatic systems, including the role of microscopic algae and bacteria in the carbon cycle of sea ice. To investigate this, he and his team collect ice cores and samples of the salty brine that sits between the individual ice crystals. The brine collects at the bottom of the hole from which the core is taken, about half a metre down, and it can be difficult to retrieve. The hole is too deep to stick an arm down, and when the team tried using syringes, they clogged and broke in the extreme cold.

“We’ve found over the years that a soup ladle attached to a pole is the ideal thing,” Thomas says, adding that once the brine is collected, another kitchen tool — a strainer — helps to remove lingering ice crystals that would otherwise dilute the sample. “You can get very technical sieves, of course, but the one in my kitchen is perfect in that it’s robust, cheap and easy to sterilize.”

At a time when there seems to be a niche scientific ‘unitasker’ for every process, getting back to basics and repurposing, or even developing, simple tools offers a sense of freedom. With a little creativity, scientists are building surprisingly robust kits to aid tasks in fields from microscopy to oceanography. Their experiences highlight what research do-it-yourself enthusiasts have long understood: that compelling science doesn’t always require the most-complex equipment, or the most expensive.

A spirit of improvisation

Thomas’s work in polar systems is an extreme example, but the fact remains that fieldwork demands a certain level of thinking on your feet, particularly when working in remote areas with little support. “When something breaks, you usually have to make do with what you have on hand,” Thomas says. “A spirit of improvisation is essential in a good field scientist.”

As a result, field researchers often travel with essentials such as bundles of zip ties and rolls of duct tape for on-the-fly repairs. But beyond these universal items, some researchers have developed or adopted common, low-tech solutions that are tailored to the specifics of their work.

Creative workarounds

Kristina Young, an ecologist at the University of Wisconsin–Madison, studies the soil of dryland ecosystems, which cover 40% of Earth’s land area, excluding Antarctica. With little plant cover to break wind and water flow, the microtopography in these areas — or how bumpy the surface is — can be an important driver of processes such as dust deposition, seed sprouting and erosion. Young says that she’s used a handful of seemingly strange tools in her work, including a BB gun and a jewellery chain. Shooting a pellet into the ground helps her to gauge the susceptibility of the soil to wind erosion, and draping a chain over the ground’s surface and measuring its length provides an estimate of soil roughness. “These are pretty crude estimates,” Young admits, “but they still tell us a surprising amount about the ecosystem.”

Modern tools, such as drones, can make the same measurements, sometimes with greater precision, but the chain is much cheaper and easier to travel with. Getting permission to fly a drone is often difficult, or even impossible in some countries, Young says. And drones can complicate reproducibility if collaborators don’t have access to the same tools. Young is part of an international effort called CrustNet, in which researchers use shared protocols to study drylands, and the chain method is one of several simple metrics that have helped the project to expand.

“We’ve been able to launch a global collaboration purely by basing protocols around things that most people can access and learn to use quickly,” Young says, adding: “We’d love to study every site in really deep detail, but the reality is that we have to be practical about what works. For this project, the jewellery chain is the correct tool for the job.”

David Thomas uses a soup ladle on a pole to collect brine samples in the Arctic.Credit: David N. Thomas

Accessibility and reproducibility also form the foundation of Saša Iskrić’s work as part of KAP Jasa, a non-profit kite organization in Ljubljana, Slovenia. The group isn’t focused on science, but Iskrić says that they have sometimes assisted on research projects, such as an aerial survey of a newly discovered Roman villa, imaging remnants of medieval agriculture and assessing water pollution in the Ljubljanica River.

Rapid 3D-printing method could be used on space missions

The group’s most common kite for these studies is a rokkaku dako, a six-sided structure of Japanese design. Around 1.8 metres tall and 1.5 metres wide, these large kites can carry more than one kilogram, and Iskrić predicts that people will send microphones, meteorological equipment or even Geiger counters aloft to help answer research questions.

Kites offer several benefits compared with drones, Iskrić says. “A kite can stay aloft for hours, while a simple drone flies for half an hour before its batteries get depleted,” he says, adding that this problem is greatly exacerbated once you start adding weight. “Also, one can’t fly [drones] in urban areas, in national parks or over heritage or archaeological sites. But, one can fly a kite pretty much anywhere.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights how simple, accessible household tools and low-tech solutions are enabling scientific research to be more reproducible, affordable, and practical, especially in challenging environments. This approach broadens participation in research, supports global collaborations, and overcomes logistical and financial barriers, thus having a meaningful positive impact on the scientific community and society.
articles.personality_title: Everyday tools help scientists solve tough problems in the field
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientific research often uses high-tech equipment, but in some cases, simple household items can be just as helpful, especially when working in tough environments like the Arctic or drylands.

**What happened** – Researchers like David Thomas in Finland use kitchen tools such as soup ladles and strainers to collect samples from sea ice where high-tech tools fail in extreme cold. In the US, ecologist Kristina Young uses a BB gun and a jewellery chain to study soil erosion and surface roughness in drylands, providing useful data without expensive equipment. In Slovenia, the group KAP Jasa uses large kites to carry scientific instruments for surveys, offering a longer flying time and fewer restrictions than drones.

**Impact** – These simple tools make scientific work more practical, affordable, and accessible. They allow researchers to gather data in remote or difficult places without needing costly or fragile technology. This approach also helps scientists around the world work together by using tools and methods that anyone can access, improving the reliability and reach of research.

**What's next step** – Scientists will likely continue to develop and share creative, low-tech solutions for fieldwork. This could lead to more global collaborations and discoveries, especially in places where technology is hard to use or too expensive. Groups like CrustNet show how shared simple methods can expand research across many countries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using everyday tools in science helps researchers overcome challenges, making fieldwork easier, cheaper, and more open to collaboration worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des outils du quotidien aident les scientifiques à relever des défis sur le terrain
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La recherche scientifique utilise souvent des équipements high-tech, mais dans certains cas, des objets simples du quotidien peuvent être tout aussi utiles, surtout dans des environnements difficiles comme l’Arctique ou les zones arides.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs comme David Thomas en Finlande utilisent des ustensiles de cuisine, comme des louches et des passoires, pour prélever des échantillons dans la glace de mer, là où les outils sophistiqués échouent dans le froid extrême. Aux États-Unis, l’écologue Kristina Young utilise un pistolet à billes et une chaîne de bijoux pour étudier l’érosion des sols et la rugosité du terrain dans les zones sèches, fournissant des données utiles sans équipement coûteux. En Slovénie, le groupe KAP Jasa emploie de grands cerfs-volants pour transporter des instruments scientifiques lors de relevés, offrant un temps de vol plus long et moins de restrictions que les drones.

**Impact** – Ces outils simples rendent le travail scientifique plus pratique, abordable et accessible. Ils permettent aux chercheurs de collecter des données dans des lieux éloignés ou difficiles sans avoir besoin de technologies coûteuses ou fragiles. Cette approche aide aussi les scientifiques du monde entier à collaborer en utilisant des outils et méthodes accessibles à tous, améliorant la fiabilité et la portée des recherches.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques continueront probablement à développer et partager des solutions créatives et peu technologiques pour le travail sur le terrain. Cela pourrait conduire à plus de collaborations internationales et de découvertes, surtout dans les endroits où la technologie est difficile à utiliser ou trop chère. Des groupes comme CrustNet montrent comment des méthodes simples partagées peuvent étendre la recherche dans de nombreux pays.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Utiliser des outils du quotidien en science aide les chercheurs à surmonter les défis, rendant le travail sur le terrain plus facile, moins cher et plus ouvert à la collaboration mondiale.
articles.personality_title_es: Herramientas cotidianas ayudan a científicos a resolver problemas difíciles en el campo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La investigación científica a menudo usa equipos de alta tecnología, pero en algunos casos, objetos simples del hogar pueden ser igual de útiles, especialmente en ambientes difíciles como el Ártico o las zonas secas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores como David Thomas en Finlandia usan utensilios de cocina como cucharones y coladores para recoger muestras en el hielo marino donde las herramientas sofisticadas fallan por el frío extremo. En Estados Unidos, la ecóloga Kristina Young usa una pistola de balines y una cadena de joyería para estudiar la erosión del suelo y la rugosidad del terreno en zonas secas, proporcionando datos útiles sin equipo caro. En Eslovenia, el grupo KAP Jasa utiliza grandes cometas para llevar instrumentos científicos en estudios, ofreciendo más tiempo de vuelo y menos restricciones que los drones.

**Impacto** – Estas herramientas simples hacen que el trabajo científico sea más práctico, accesible y económico. Permiten a los investigadores obtener datos en lugares remotos o difíciles sin necesidad de tecnología costosa o frágil. Este enfoque también ayuda a científicos de todo el mundo a colaborar usando métodos y herramientas al alcance de todos, mejorando la confiabilidad y el alcance de la investigación.

**Próximo paso** – Es probable que los científicos sigan desarrollando y compartiendo soluciones creativas y de baja tecnología para el trabajo de campo. Esto podría llevar a más colaboraciones globales y descubrimientos, especialmente en lugares donde la tecnología es difícil de usar o demasiado cara. Proyectos como CrustNet muestran cómo métodos simples compartidos pueden ampliar la investigación en muchos países.

**Resumen en una frase** – Usar herramientas cotidianas en la ciencia ayuda a los investigadores a superar desafíos, haciendo que el trabajo de campo sea más fácil, barato y abierto a la colaboración mundial.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a rustic Arctic field scene featuring a long pole with a soup ladle scooping briny water from a hole in the ice, alongside a simple kitchen strainer resting nearby on the snow; in the background, a gently undulating dryland landscape with a delicate jewelry chain draped over textured soil, and a large traditional rokkaku kite soaring steadily above, all rendered with natural, muted colors and soft light.

articles.title: Inside the start-up aiming for a giant leap in robot intelligence
articles.summary: Physical Intelligence is drawing on the broad knowledge of large language models to help robots understand instructions and learn to carry out any task independently
articles.content:
Physical Intelligence is drawing on the broad knowledge of large language models to help robots understand instructions and learn to carry out any task independently

A robot powered by Physical Intelligence’s AI folding laundry Physical Intelligence

In San Francisco, inside a warehouse coated with gleaming steel panels, I am handed a fresh cup of coffee made entirely by a robot. This fact alone is unimpressive — robots have been making coffee for more than a decade — but the robotic brain that made this coffee is no one-trick pony. It has also learned how to do many other tasks, such as folding clothes, peeling vegetables and cleaning kitchens, in the time that most toddlers barely learn how to walk.

Physical Intelligence, a start-up founded in 2024, is betting that a robot brain that can learn how to do many different tasks will, in the not-too-distant future, enable robots to become enmeshed in our daily lives. Instead of focusing on a single machine, like the humanoid robots built by Tesla or Boston Dynamics or the factory robots used by Amazon, the company wants to build an adaptable control system that can perform many tasks with many different machines.

A general-purpose robotic intelligence isn’t a new idea: many roboticists would say it has been a long-term goal for decades. But, just as the early 2020s saw a flourishing of the large language models (LLMs) that power AI chatbots thanks to the right combination of computing power, data and algorithmic advances, Physical Intelligence is hoping to conjure a similar leap of progress in general robotics.

“In most domains, solving more problems only makes things harder, but in AI, it actually makes it easier, because then you have more diverse sources of knowledge to learn from,” says Sergey Levine at the University of California, Berkeley, who is one of the firm’s founders.

The success of LLMs has led to a new kind of robotic AI, called a vision-language-action (VLA) model, that underpins much of Physical Intelligence’s research. Instead of teaching a robot one skill at a time, a VLA uses the broad knowledge of an LLM to translate general requests into specific actions, enabling robots to follow instructions and carry out many different tasks. “[VLAs] are probably the most direct translation of the excitement that we have from large language models,” says Ingmar Posner at the University of Oxford. Rather than predict the next word, these systems predict the next robotic move needed to complete a particular task, he says.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Daily The latest on what’s new in science and why it matters each day. Sign up to newsletter

One big challenge for training robots is that there is a near-infinite number of real-world variations for any task, and very little data for robots to learn on. Automating the learning – teaching robots to teach themselves – is a possible solution, but most robot developers have shied away from doing this because gathering enough data is a tall order, says Levine. “Even though, in principle, it should be automatic, in practice, the amount of work required to get the data for your particular application was larger than the work needed to just do everything by hand.”

Levine and his colleagues are hoping that, powered by VLAs, they will need considerably less data to succeed. Beneath the boardroom where I spoke with Levine, a fleet of employees were teaching the robots to do what appeared to be a banal series of tasks: folding shirts, placing pillows on shelves, cutting bows on present boxes. Around the corner, I learned, were two warehouses containing fake supermarkets, bedrooms and kitchens, which were renovated each week, where Physical Intelligence’s robots and AI models could learn to cope with a variety of settings. The company was also rolling its robots out to real, lived-in homes to test how they might cope with the mess of the real world.

Physical Intelligence’s building in San Francisco ALEX WILKINS

This variety is one of the key ingredients that has led to a surprising amount of progress, including robots learning to generalise beyond tasks they have seen before. A recent model, called π0.7, was able to cook sweet potatoes in an air fryer with step-by-step verbal instructions from a human, despite never having used an air fryer before.

The speed of progress in the two years that Physical Intelligence has been operating has surprised Levine. “It’s actually gone quite a bit quicker than we thought,” he says.

Other companies are taking notice. A slew of start-ups with billions of dollars in funding, as well as more established companies, like Amazon and Google DeepMind, are attempting to develop their own general-purpose robots.

Despite the rapid advances so far, it is hard to predict how fast the field will move forward. Progress for AI companies like OpenAI and Anthropic has famously been rapid, but the going is often slower for robotics companies. Every robotics researcher will be familiar with Moravec’s paradox: computer scientist Hans Moravec observed in 1988 that it is easy for robots to master games like chess or score highly on IQ tests, yet it is “difficult or impossible to give them the skills of a one-year-old when it comes to perception and mobility”.

It is still unclear just how much data Physical Intelligence will need to prepare its robots for real-world use, says Posner. “I would say right now we’re at early signs that something interesting might be happening, but whether that’s really the route to go is a different question.”

He thinks success in the real world is still a long way off, partly because users will push robots to their limits. “Humans are adversarial. They like messing with a robot, if nothing else, just because it’s fun,” says Posner. “Do I believe this stuff is going to get deployed at scale anytime soon, with a business model that actually makes money? No, definitely not. I would find that very difficult to believe.”
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological advancement in robotics AI, where a start-up is developing general-purpose robot intelligence using large language models to enable robots to perform diverse tasks autonomously. This progress has broad implications for integrating robots into daily life, potentially benefiting society by automating routine tasks and improving efficiency. The article provides detailed context about the technology, its challenges, and its potential impact, meeting criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Start-up develops robot brain that learns many tasks like folding laundry and cooking
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** –
Physical Intelligence is a new company in San Francisco working on a special kind of robot brain. Unlike traditional robots that do only one job, this robot brain uses advanced language AI to understand and perform many different tasks.

**What happened** –
Founded in 2024, Physical Intelligence uses large language models (LLMs), similar to those behind chatbots, to teach robots how to follow general instructions. Their robots can fold clothes, make coffee, peel vegetables, and even cook using new tools like air fryers by following spoken step-by-step directions. They train their robots in realistic fake homes and real houses to prepare them for everyday life.

**Impact** –
This approach is unique because it allows robots to learn many tasks without needing huge amounts of data for each one. Instead of programming a robot for every job, the robot’s AI can figure out what to do from general knowledge. This could make robots more useful at home and work, handling many chores instead of only one specific task.

**What's next step** –
Physical Intelligence and other companies will keep improving these robot brains and testing them in real-world settings. While experts say it might take time before robots are widely used and reliable, the progress so far shows promise. The company plans to continue teaching robots new skills and making them adapt to messy, unpredictable environments.

**One-sentence takeaway** –
A new start-up is using language AI to create robot brains that can learn and carry out many household tasks, moving robots closer to everyday use.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une start-up crée un cerveau robot capable d’apprendre plusieurs tâches comme plier le linge et cuisiner
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** –
Physical Intelligence est une nouvelle entreprise de San Francisco qui développe un cerveau robot spécial. Contrairement aux robots traditionnels qui font un seul travail, ce cerveau utilise une intelligence artificielle avancée pour comprendre et réaliser plusieurs tâches différentes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** –
Fondée en 2024, Physical Intelligence utilise de grands modèles de langage (LLM), semblables à ceux des chatbots, pour apprendre aux robots à suivre des instructions générales. Leurs robots peuvent plier des vêtements, préparer du café, éplucher des légumes, et même cuisiner avec de nouveaux appareils comme la friteuse à air en suivant des instructions orales étape par étape. Ils entraînent leurs robots dans des maisons factices réalistes et dans de vraies maisons pour les préparer à la vie quotidienne.

**Impact** –
Cette méthode est unique car elle permet aux robots d’apprendre plusieurs tâches sans avoir besoin de beaucoup de données pour chacune. Au lieu de programmer un robot pour chaque travail, l’IA du robot peut comprendre ce qu’il faut faire à partir de connaissances générales. Cela pourrait rendre les robots plus utiles à la maison et au travail, en réalisant plusieurs corvées au lieu d’une seule tâche spécifique.

**Prochaine étape** –
Physical Intelligence et d’autres entreprises vont continuer à améliorer ces cerveaux robotiques et à les tester dans des environnements réels. Même si les experts pensent que cela prendra du temps avant que les robots soient largement utilisés et fiables, les progrès réalisés sont prometteurs. L’entreprise prévoit d’apprendre de nouvelles compétences aux robots et de les faire s’adapter à des environnements imprévisibles.

**Résumé en une phrase** –
Une start-up utilise l’IA du langage pour créer des cerveaux robotiques capables d’apprendre et d’exécuter plusieurs tâches ménagères, rapprochant les robots de l’usage quotidien.
articles.personality_title_es: Start-up crea cerebro robótico que aprende tareas como doblar ropa y cocinar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** –
Physical Intelligence es una nueva empresa en San Francisco que está desarrollando un cerebro especial para robots. A diferencia de los robots tradicionales que hacen solo una tarea, este cerebro usa inteligencia artificial avanzada para entender y realizar muchas tareas diferentes.

**Qué pasó** –
Fundada en 2024, Physical Intelligence utiliza grandes modelos de lenguaje (LLM), similares a los que usan los chatbots, para enseñar a los robots a seguir instrucciones generales. Sus robots pueden doblar ropa, preparar café, pelar verduras e incluso cocinar con herramientas nuevas como freidoras de aire siguiendo instrucciones habladas paso a paso. Entrenan a sus robots en casas falsas realistas y en hogares reales para prepararlos para la vida diaria.

**Impacto** –
Este método es único porque permite que los robots aprendan muchas tareas sin necesitar grandes cantidades de datos para cada una. En lugar de programar un robot para cada trabajo, la inteligencia artificial del robot puede entender qué hacer a partir de conocimientos generales. Esto podría hacer que los robots sean más útiles en casa y en el trabajo, realizando muchas tareas en lugar de solo una específica.

**Próximo paso** –
Physical Intelligence y otras compañías seguirán mejorando estos cerebros robóticos y probándolos en entornos reales. Aunque los expertos dicen que podría tomar tiempo para que los robots sean usados ampliamente y sean confiables, el progreso hasta ahora es prometedor. La empresa planea continuar enseñando nuevas habilidades a los robots y hacer que se adapten a ambientes desordenados e impredecibles.

**Resumen en una frase** –
Una start-up usa IA de lenguaje para crear cerebros robóticos que pueden aprender y realizar muchas tareas del hogar, acercando a los robots al uso cotidiano.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sleek, friendly robot with multiple articulated arms gently folding colorful clothes and placing a steaming cup of coffee on a wooden table inside a softly lit, modern warehouse filled with minimalist shelves and simple household items, symbolizing versatile robotic intelligence learning diverse everyday tasks through subtle glowing lines connecting the robot’s “brain” to the objects it interacts with.
